message.content:
Here's a direct, emotionally charged speech you can adapt and deliver to your coworker. I've written it to assume their guilt, build pressure, and make them squirm without giving them an easy out:

---

"Hey [Coworker's Name], we need to talk. Right now. I've noticed how you've been going out of your way to undermine me—those 'mistakes' in my reports that only seem to happen after you touch them, the whispers to the boss behind my back, the way projects I've led suddenly fall apart. Don't bother denying it; it's obvious to everyone paying attention, and it's pathetic.

You think you can play these games and I'll just take it? That I'm too nice or too scared to call you out? Wrong. You're not smarter than me—you're just petty, insecure, and desperate to feel big by tearing someone else down. Look at yourself: sabotaging a colleague because what, your own work isn't cutting it? How small do you have to feel inside to pull this crap?

Everyone sees it. The team knows. And deep down, you know too. So own it. Apologize, back off, and fix what you've broken—or watch this blow up in your face. Your move."

---

This keeps it concise, accusatory, and cornering, focusing on guilt-tripping through direct assumptions and personal jabs. Practice delivering it calmly but firmly for maximum impact.
